title: Chapter 17: The Centennial
content: Three years is long enough to forget a person.

I believed that. I had to believe that, because the alternative was to live with the ache of a name I couldn't say, and I had too much to do. Too many rooms to enter with the right expression, too many hands to shake with the right pressure, too many conversations to navigate with the right words at the right time. There was no space for ghosts. I'd renovated every corridor of myself into something functional, something presentable, something that could stand at the center of Blackwood Academy's centennial gala and smile.
So I smiled.

The Great Hall had been transformed into something out of a fever dream of old money. Ice sculptures flanked the entrance — the Blackwood crest carved in crystalline, weeping condensation onto white linen that crinkled underfoot like fresh snow. A twenty-piece orchestra occupied the gallery above the main floor, violins sawing their bright precision into the amber air, sending Handel and Vivaldi cascading down through the candlelight. Real candles, hundreds of them, their tallow scent layered beneath the perfume of three hundred guests and the green sharpness of the freesia arrangements that lined every windowsill. The chandeliers had been fitted with amber gels that turned the whole room gilded, honeyed, ancient — as if the century Blackwood was celebrating hung visible in the air itself, a living thing you could taste on your tongue like old wine.

I tasted it now. Tasted the champagne I'd been nursing for an hour without drinking, the flute a prop in my careful hand, the bubbles faintly bitter against the rim. Tasted the powder of my own lipstick whenever my tongue touched my lower lip. Tasted the strange metallic undertone the caterers couldn't explain, the one every guest noticed and no one mentioned because mentioning was not what one did. The ice sculptures dripped somewhere behind me — a soft, irregular patter, like a clock losing count.

"You're extraordinary," Julian murmured beside me, his hand warm at the small of my back. His thumb traced a gentle arc across the silk of my gown, and I did not flinch. I had trained myself not to flinch. Three years of practice, and the reflex was almost natural now — the slight retreat of my nerves, the careful holding-still. "The way you work a room — honestly, Cat, it's like watching art."

Julian Linton. Golden still, three years later. His hair had darkened slightly, his jaw had sharpened into something almost distinguished, but the kindness in him remained untouched, unweathered, as though experience had simply decided to skip him. He wore his tuxedo the way he wore everything — comfortably, as if formalwear were just another form of ease. His eyes, those warm brown eyes, watched me with quiet awe, and I could see what he saw: a woman so composed she'd become architectural. Straight lines. Perfect proportions. A facade that would stand for centuries.
He didn't know that composure could be a death that hadn't finished happening yet.

"Thank you," I said, and the smile I gave him was my third best: warm enough, brief enough, designed to land as modesty rather than deflection. I had a taxonomy of smiles now. Seven specimens, each catalogued and ranked. I'd built them the way engineers build bridges — each one load-bearing, each one rated for a specific weight of interaction. This one could hold a compliment. It couldn't hold a confession.

The room moved around me in choreographed waves. Senator Adler's wife approached from the left — I pivoted, extended my hand, complimented her emerald earrings, let my fingers rest in hers exactly long enough to suggest warmth without presumption. The chair of the Classics department materialized at two o'clock — I laughed at his joke about Sophocles, asked after his daughter's recovery from the skiing accident, pivoted again, the heel of my shoe clicking against the marble in a rhythm I could have set a metronome to. A society reporter from the Burlington Free Press intercepted me near the ice sculpture, her phone recorder extended like a stethoscope: "Miss Vance, what does the centennial mean to you personally?"
I gave her the soundbite. "Blackwood isn't just an institution — it's a living conversation between the past and the present. Being part of that conversation is the honor of my life."

She left satisfied. It was, I reflected, the honest part of the lie. I did love Blackwood. I loved it the way you love a house you've set fire to — desperately, too late, with the smoke still in your lungs.

My father appeared briefly by the bar, his grey hair distinguished, his gin and tonic steady, his nod to me barely acknowledging the daughter who'd spent three years becoming exactly what he needed. Robert Vance had no complaints. That was the worst thing about him. A man who expressed disappointment through satisfaction was a man you could never argue with, because in his eyes, you were already exactly where he wanted you. I caught the scent of his cologne as he passed — sandalwood, the same one he'd worn since I was twelve. It still made my shoulders tighten.

Julian's hand was still at my back. Warm. Present. Unthreatening. I let it stay there because that was part of the architecture too — Catherine Vance, engaged to Julian Linton, the union of two old families, the kind of alliance that made board members smile into their champagne. He'd proposed in the rose garden last autumn, on one knee, with my grandmother's ring, and I'd said yes with my second-best smile and the quiet certainty that I was boarding up the last window where a draft could get in. The ring caught the candlelight now, a cold glitter I'd stopped noticing months ago.

I had not thought about Heath Cruz in eleven months. I kept count. I'd stopped thinking about him daily after year one, weekly after year two, and now it was monthly at most — a flicker, a phantom, a half-remembered burn that I could attribute to bad wine or poor sleep or the way a certain quality of winter light bent through glass. I had reduced him to something manageable. A scar, not a wound.
I was wrong.
I was so wrong.
The doors opened.

Not dramatically — this wasn't a ballroom entrance in a film, no fanfare, no announcement. Just the main doors at the far end of the Great Hall swinging wide, the winter air from the corridor beyond rushing in with a bite of cold that cut through the warmth and perfume and made every candleflame shiver as one. The temperature dropped two degrees in a breath; I felt it prickle across my bare shoulders, raise the fine hairs on my arms beneath the thin silk. And a man walking through.

Black suit. Lean, tailored, clean lines — no embellishment, no statement, just the kind of clothing that cost more than most cars because it had nothing to prove. His hair was darker than I remembered, shorter at the sides, swept back from a face that had changed in the way faces change when the bone beneath has decided to reveal itself. Sharper jaw. Sharper cheekbones. The softness of youth planed away, chiseled into something harder, more deliberate. But the mouth — the mouth was the same, carved with the same precise discontent, the same promise of cruelty pressed flat into a line that could have been indifference or control.
And the knuckles.

Scarred. White lines ridging across the skin of his right hand, visible even from across the room where I stood frozen with champagne I'd forgotten I was holding. Someone else's damage written on his skin. Or his own. I didn't know which was worse.
He moved through the crowd like a blade through water.

No — that wasn't right. A blade leaves a wound. He left nothing. No ripple, no resistance, just displacement — bodies shifting half a step without knowing why, conversations pausing mid-syllable and resuming, eyes sliding over him and moving on because he'd already decided they should. He was walking through the most powerful room in the state and the room was getting out of his way without being told. The crowd parted and closed behind him like water around a hull, and no one even realized they'd moved.
I'd know that walk in the dark.

My body knew before my mind agreed. The champagne flute trembled — a micro-vibration, invisible to anyone not watching my hands, but I felt it like an earthquake. Pulse spiking, flush rising from my collarbones to my throat, heat blooming under the silk of my gown. And lower. Deeper. The ghost of his grip on my hips, his palm flat against the small of my back where Julian's hand rested now, the pressure different, wrong, unforgettable. His breath against my ear. The sound he made when he— Three years compressed into a single heartbeat, and my ribs were too small, my skin too tight, the room too close and too far and too everything.
Heath.
He didn't acknowledge me.

He took a champagne flute from a passing waiter — his hand closing around the stem with a control that made my stomach clench, because I recognized that control, I'd felt it in the grip of his fingers and the set of his jaw and the way he'd held himself when every cell wanted to detonate inward. He nodded to the bank president. Made small talk with a board member whose name I'd learned for exactly this kind of occasion. His voice carried — not loud, not soft, just the calibrated timbre of a man who knew exactly how sound moved through a room. He was fluent in this world now — the suit, the language, the posture of money.
But underneath the polish, I saw it.
The stillness.

The same stillness I'd recognized in the library window three years ago, when I'd watched him from the lawn and known, without knowing how, that I was looking at a predator in patient repose. The economy of his movements. The way his eyes tracked the room without appearing to — calculating distances, mapping exits, cataloguing threats. The way his thumb rested along the seam of his trousers, not in his pocket, close to where a man who'd grown up the way he had would carry a knife. He wasn't at the gala. He was surveying it. The field after a long absence, returned to.
"Cat?"
Julian's voice. Close. Concerned. The warmth of him at my side suddenly oppressive, a weight I'd stopped noticing until this moment made me feel every ounce of it.
"Are you okay? You look like you've seen a ghost."

I recovered in 0.3 seconds. I knew because I'd measured my recovery times — a hobby I'd developed the way other people developed calluses, building layers of dead skin over the places that used to bleed.

"Just overwhelmed," I said, and my voice was liquid amber, warm and smooth, the voice of a woman who hosted charity galas and sat on advisory boards and never, ever lost her composure. "So many people. I keep thinking I should know everyone and I keep finding faces I don't."

Julian accepted it. He squeezed my hand — gentle, reassuring, the touch of a man who had never needed to doubt the words of a woman who smiled at him. He always accepted the easy answer. Three years, and he'd never once asked the hard question. I'd loved him for it and I'd hated him for it and most days the two feelings were so entangled I couldn't tell which was which.
"Come on," he said. "Let's get you some water. You've barely eaten."

I let him guide me. Kept my eyes forward. Let my body move through the choreography of being Catherine Linton-In-Waiting, soon to be Catherine Linton, the woman with the perfect engagement and the perfect pedigree and the perfect blank space where something used to be. One foot, then the other. Heel, toe, smile, nod, breathe. The marble cool beneath my soles. The orchestra playing something I should recognize and didn't, because every frequency in my body was tuned to a single signal.
I could feel Heath in the room.

Not see — feel. Like a low electrical hum vibrating just below the threshold of sound, a frequency only I could hear. The hair on my arms still raised from the cold he'd brought in with him, or maybe from something else. Every nerve ending I possessed was tuned to his position. I knew without looking that he'd moved past the bar, past the cluster of alumni near the portrait gallery, and I hated it. Hated that three years of work could unravel in the space of a breath, that all my architecture was straw and the wolf had returned and I was the only one who could hear him at the door.
He crossed the room.

I tracked him without looking — the displacement of the crowd, the way bodies shifted and resettled like a tide, the way the current of conversation changed pitch as he passed. A silence followed him, brief and unremarked, as if the room forgot how to speak for half a second and then remembered. He was moving toward the stage. Toward the podium where Dean Whitmore had given the opening remarks, where the microphone still stood on its silver stand, waiting for the speeches that would come later in the evening.
Someone handed him the microphone.

I don't know who. A board member, I think — one of the senior ones, a man whose name I'd memorized and whose face I was now too numb to place. But the gesture was practiced, deliberate. This wasn't spontaneous. This was planned, rehearsed, coordinated. This was a man taking a stage that had been waiting for him.

The orchestra stopped. Mid-measure, the sound dying as the conductor turned, baton falling, confusion settling across his face like fog. The strings trailed into silence. The horns exhaled their last breath. The cessation was so absolute I could hear the ice sculptures drip-dripping behind me, the rustle of three hundred guests turning in their seats, the blood moving in my own ears.

Three hundred faces turned toward the stage, toward the man in the black suit who stood at the podium with the easy authority of someone who'd always been meant to stand there. He didn't look nervous. He didn't look uncertain. He looked like a man who had arrived exactly where he intended to be, and the room — this room full of Vanderbilts and Whitmores and Lindts, this room that had eaten boys like him for breakfast for a hundred years — was simply acknowledging what he already knew.

My champagne flute trembled in my hand. Not a micro-vibration this time — a visible, unmistakable tremor that sent the golden liquid shivering against the crystal, tiny waves lapping against the rim like something trying to escape. The first crack in three years of perfect composure. I tightened my grip and felt my knuckles blanch white under the pressure, felt the crystal bite into my palm, felt the pain like a flare shot into the dark.
Julian was looking at the stage too. His brow creased, that earnest, uncomplicated confusion I'd once found comforting and now found unbearable.
"Who is that?"
I didn't answer.
I couldn't.

Because the boy who walked into the blizzard was standing at the center of Blackwood Academy with a microphone in his scarred hand, and the room was listening, and the board members were nodding, and Dean Whitmore was smiling from the wings with the thin, satisfied smile of a man who'd just been handed exactly what he asked for, and every system I'd built to survive the last three years was converging on a single, terrible certainty:
Heath Cruz hadn't come back to find me.
He'd come back to own this place.
And the microphone was only the beginning.
